message.content:
The key is to balance clinical detail, human emotion, and restraint. Here’s a sample scene you can adapt, followed by a quick breakdown of choices to keep it historical and not gratuitous.

---

They laid him on the rough trestle table that had once been a door, its planks warped and stained the color of old wine. The air in the tent was thick with steam and the sharp, sour tang of blood and vinegar. Outside, the muffled roar of the battlefield drifted in—drums, shouted orders, the distant bray of trumpets—blunted by canvas and smoke.

“Hold him,” said Master Arnulf.

Two orderlies pressed the soldier’s shoulders and hips down, another pinned the shattered leg. The limb below the knee was a ruin of crushed bone and torn flesh, the boot bulging unnaturally, leather dark and stiff where blood had soaked through and dried in spots. Arnulf had seen worse, but he still allowed himself a heartbeat to take its measure, judging how much could be spared and where life ended and rot would soon begin.

He met the man’s eyes. “It will be quick, if you help me,” Arnulf said. “Bite on this. Do not pull away.”

He pushed a rolled strip of leather between the soldier’s teeth. The man’s face was gray beneath the grime, sweat already beading on his brow despite the cold. His hands clawed at the edges of the table until one of the orderlies pried them free and gripped them tight.

Arnulf’s apprentice passed him the long knife, its edge honed bright. The boy had tried to wipe away the last patient’s blood, but a faint rust-colored shadow remained along the spine of the blade. Arnulf did not look away from the leg. He ran his fingers quickly above the knee, feeling for the hard rail of bone, for the yielding runnels where the great vessels pulsed. He marked his place with thumb and forefinger.

“On my word,” he told the men holding the patient. “Now.”

He cut fast, drawing the knife in one clean crescent through skin and the thick layer of yellow fat beneath. The patient’s whole body arched, a raw, animal sound tearing out of him, low at first, then climbing into a hoarse scream around the leather gag. Blood welled up at once, bright and sudden, coursing down over Arnulf’s knuckles and spilling in hot streams onto the table, then to the packed earth floor.

“Hold him,” the surgeon snapped. “Do not let him buck.”

He deepened the cut, slicing through muscle in short, efficient strokes, exposing the pale sheen of bone and the bundled, ropy cords of tendon above it. Each pull of the knife drew a fresh cry from the man, his throat already ragged, his legs thrashing until the orderlies pinned them with their full weight. The smell of opened flesh—metallic, almost sweet—rose thick in the tent.

Arnulf worked without pause. His hands were steady, movements practiced, almost indifferent. Around the leg, the meat of the thigh separated in broad red tongues, which he pulled back with hooks so that the flap would later cover the stump. A spurting artery painted the underside of his sleeve scarlet before he caught it with his fingers and crushed it, feeling the vessel jump against his grip.

“Thread,” he called.

The apprentice’s hands shook as he passed a needle already armed with coarse silk. Arnulf pinched the end of the artery with iron forceps and ran the needle through, knotting it off in a swift, practiced motion. The thin jet of blood slowed, then ceased to beat. He found the second vessel, then the smaller branches, tying each with the same ruthless economy.

The soldier’s screams had broken into uneven sobs, his chest heaving, tears cutting rivulets through the grime on his face. His gaze, unfocused, darted to the canvas roof where a fly crawled lazily in a beam of light. The leather in his mouth was dark with saliva and flecks of blood where he had bitten through his own lip.

“Almost there,” Arnulf said, though he doubted the man could hear him now.

He exchanged the knife for the bone saw, its teeth fine but hungry. The first kiss of metal on bone set off a new, strangled howl, the sound tearing out of the soldier as if from somewhere deeper than his lungs. The saw rasped, a harsh, grating music that vibrated up Arnulf’s arm. White bone dust mixed with blood into a pink paste that spattered his apron and flecked his hands. The leg jolted with each stroke, the restraints creaking as the orderlies fought to keep the man still.

“Steady,” Arnulf murm
